title: Relationships, Marriage, and Family / Work and the Economy
summary:
Florida Chapter 9 merges two full original chapters into a single condensed chapter: Chapter 14 (Relationships, Marriage, and Family) and Chapter 18 (Work and the Economy). Both halves are significantly abridged, with much of the original analytical and narrative depth removed in favor of summarized prose. The family sections retain core factual content but compress multi-page discussions into brief paragraphs, stripping out sidebars, learning objectives, section quizzes, short-answer questions, and further-reading resources.

The most consequential deletions in the family half concern LGBTQ+ content and same-sex marriage. The original chapter included a dedicated subsection on same-sex couples that discussed the positive impact of marriage legalization on LGBTQ+ mental health and teen suicide rates; the Florida version retains the demographic statistics on same-sex couples but removes every passage discussing psychological benefits, LGBTQ+ acceptance, and the suicide study. The extended discussion of transgender people and their elevated IPV victimization rates is also entirely deleted. The 'Evolution of Television Families' sidebar, which prominently featured gay characters and diverse family structures, is removed. The entire child abuse section, including data on prevalence, shaken-baby syndrome, corporal punishment, and long-term consequences, is cut from the Florida version.

The economic half suffers even more dramatic cuts. The Florida version entirely omits the 'Economic Systems' section (18.1), which covered the history of capitalism and socialism, the Industrial Revolution, labor unions, convergence theory, Karl Marx and conflict theory applied to economies, and the 'Politicians, Socialism, and Changing Perspectives' sidebar that discussed democratic socialism and income inequality. Also removed are the 'Women in the Workforce' subsection (which noted persistent wage inequality), the specific passage attributing class mobility barriers to connections and social capital, a 'Real Money, Virtual Worlds' sidebar, and the Detroit bankruptcy case study. Several critical-perspective framings are softened or deleted. The Florida version adds new content on the gender wage gap (with updated 2024 data), immigration policy under the Trump administration, and a Florida-specific minimum wage comparison.

changes.original_text: In the early 2000s, the nontraditional family has become somewhat of a tradition in television... they include unmarried and divorced parents, adopted children, gay or lesbian couples, and multigenerational households. In 2009, ABC emphasized the changes in family dynamics with their choice of title for Modern Family. The show follows an extended family that includes a divorced and remarried father... the other who is a gay man in a committed relationship raising an adopted daughter.
changes.context: The entire 'Evolution of Television Families' sidebar is removed. This sidebar discussed the history of family representation on TV, specifically including gay and lesbian couples and nontraditional families.

changes.original_text: In an analysis of 81 parenting studies, sociologists found no quantifiable data to support the notion that opposite-sex parenting is any better than same-sex parenting. Children of lesbian couples, however, were shown to have slightly lower rates of behavioral problems and higher rates of self-esteem (Biblarz and Stacey 2010). Prior to the nationwide legalization, studies also showed that the rate of suicide among high school students declined in states where same-sex marriage was legal. Suicide is the second-highest cause of death among high school students, and it is a tragic outcome for LGBTQ teenagers who feel unaccepted or vulnerable. The evidence indicates that the legalization of same-sex marriage had positive outcomes for the emotional and mental wellbeing of LGBTQ people (Johns Hopkins University 2017).
changes.context: The entire paragraph presenting research on same-sex parenting outcomes and the documented positive effect of marriage legalization on LGBTQ teen suicide rates is deleted from the Florida version. The Johns Hopkins citation remains only in the references list, not in the body text.

changes.original_text: Studies regarding intimate partner violence against transgender people are relatively limited, but several are ongoing. A meta-analysis of available information indicated that physical IPV had occurred in the lifetimes of 38 percent of transgender people, and 25 percent of transgender people had experienced sexual IPV in their lifetimes. Compared with cisgender individuals, transgender individuals were 1.7 times more likely to experience any IPV (Peitzmeier 2020).
changes.context: The entire paragraph on IPV against transgender people is deleted. The original figure caption also referenced transgender IPV data; both are removed. Florida retains LGBTQ IPV data for gay, lesbian, and bisexual people only.

changes.original_text:
Child Abuse
Children are among the most helpless victims of abuse. In 2010, there were more than 3.3 million reports of child abuse involving an estimated 5.9 million children (Child Help 2011)... Child abuse may come in several forms, the most common being neglect (78.3 percent), followed by physical abuse (10.8 percent), sexual abuse (7.6 percent), psychological maltreatment (7.6 percent)... Infants (children less than one year old) were the most victimized population... shaken-baby syndrome... Approximately 30 percent of abused children will later abuse their own children...
changes.context: The entire child abuse section — including data on prevalence, shaken-baby syndrome, corporal punishment debate, socioeconomic risk factors, and long-term effects — is removed from the Florida version. The 'Corporal Punishment' social policy sidebar is also cut.

changes.original_text: The dominant economic systems of the modern era are capitalism and socialism... Under capitalism, people invest capital (money or property invested in a business venture) in a business to produce a product or service that can be sold in a market... Socialism is an economic system in which there is government ownership (often referred to as 'state run') of goods and their production... Organized socialism never became powerful in the United States. The success of labor unions and the government in securing workers' rights, joined with the high standard of living enjoyed by most of the workforce, made socialism less appealing...
changes.context: The entire 'Economic Systems' section (original 18.1) is removed. This included definitions of capitalism and socialism, the history of the Industrial Revolution, labor unions, Karl Marx and conflict theory applied to economies, convergence theory, and multiple sidebars. This is the single largest deletion in the chapter.

changes.original_text: In most Presidential elections, as well as some state and local contests, one or more of the candidates is likely to be insulted with a term meant to evoke fear and indicate their lack of patriotism: They are called a socialist... More recently, however, some political figures seem more comfortable with the term. Most notably, Vermont Senator Bernie Sanders has accepted the term 'democratic socialist,' as has New York Representative Alexandra Ocasio-Cortez... Many people from different political backgrounds are decrying income inequality, which, as has been described elsewhere in this text, is higher than ever in the U.S.
changes.context: The 'Politicians, Socialism, and Changing Perspectives' sidebar — which discussed democratic socialism, income inequality, and the blurred line between U.S. capitalism and socialist policies — is entirely removed.

changes.original_text: For a conflict perspective theorist, the economy is not a source of stability for society. Instead, the economy reflects and reproduces economic inequality, particularly in a capitalist marketplace. The conflict perspective is classically Marxist, with the bourgeoisie (ruling class) accumulating wealth and power by exploiting and perhaps oppressing the proletariat (workers)... Conflict theorists believe wealth is concentrated in the hands of those who do not deserve it. As of 2010, 20 percent of Americans owned 90 percent of U.S. wealth (Domhoff 2014).
changes.context: The theoretical perspectives section applying functionalism, conflict theory, and symbolic interactionism to the economy is removed. This includes the conflict perspective's discussion of Marxist class analysis, wealth concentration data, and the Occupy Wall Street reference.

changes.original_text:
Women in the Workforce
As discussed in the chapter introduction, women have been entering the workforce in ever-increasing numbers for several decades. They have also been finishing college and going on to earn higher degrees at higher rate than men do. This has resulted in many women being better positioned to obtain high-paying, high-skill jobs (Autor 2010).
While women are getting more and better jobs and their wages are rising more quickly than men's wages are, U.S. Census statistics show that they are still earning only 81 percent of what men are for the same positions (U.S. Census Bureau 2020).
changes.context: The 'Women in the Workforce' subsection is removed as a distinct section. The Florida version replaces it with a new, separately placed 'Wage Gap in the United States' section using updated 2024 data and a more neutral framing that emphasizes individual 'selection and sorting factors' over structural barriers.

changes.original_text: Increasingly, we are realizing intelligence and hard work aren't enough. If you lack knowledge of how to leverage the right names, connections, and players, you are unlikely to experience upward mobility.
changes.context: This critical observation about social capital and class barriers to meritocracy — present in the original after the meritocracy definition — is deleted from the Florida version, which retains only the neutral definition of meritocracy without the critique.

changes.original_text: Today, wealth is distributed inequitably at the top. Corporate profits have increased more than 141 percent, and CEO pay has risen by more than 298 percent.
changes.florida_text: Today, wealth is distributed unevenly at the top. Corporate profits have increased more than 141 percent, and CEO pay has risen by more than 298 percent.
changes.context: The word 'inequitably' (which carries a normative/moral judgment) is changed to the more neutral 'unevenly' in the Florida version. The factual data is retained but the editorial framing is softened.

changes.original_text: In 2005, before the recession arrived, immigrants made up a historic high of 14.7 percent of the workforce... The answer is that as of June 2009, when the National Bureau of Economic Research (NEBR) declared the recession officially over, 'foreign-born workers gained 656,000 jobs while native-born workers lost 1.2 million jobs' (Kochhar 2010)... On the other hand, undocumented immigrants tend to be trapped in extremely low-paying jobs in agriculture, service, and construction with few ways to improve their situation without risking exposure and deportation.
changes.florida_text: immigration policies shifted significantly, with efforts to reduce both legal and unauthorized immigration. These included stricter enforcement, limits on work visas such as H-1B, reductions in green cards, and curtailing refugee admissions. The administration's policies often made legal immigration more challenging and increased risks for undocumented immigrants in low-wage jobs... As of 2025, immigration policy under the Trump administration remains stringent, continuing aggressive enforcement and deportation efforts while limiting legal immigration pathways.
changes.context: The immigration and workforce section is substantially rewritten. The original's detailed labor market data for immigrant workers (including recession-era job gains) and the sympathetic framing of undocumented workers is replaced with new content on Trump administration immigration enforcement. The Florida version explicitly names the Trump administration and discusses policy restrictions.

changes.florida_text:
The Wage Gap in the United States
The Equal Pay Act, passed by the U.S. Congress in 1963, was designed to reduce the wage gap between men and women... In 2024, women working full time, year-round in wage and salary jobs earned approximately 81 cents, on average, for every dollar earned by men in the United States (U.S. Census Bureau, 2025)... The gender wage gap is wider for women of color. Black women earn around 63 to 65 cents, Latinas about 54 to 58 cents...
changes.context: A substantially expanded and repositioned 'Wage Gap' section is added near the start of the economics half, using 2024 data. While the original had a shorter wage gap discussion, the Florida version greatly expands it with nuanced discussion of occupational segregation and selection factors, while framing much of the gap as attributable to individual choices ('women often prioritize flexibility or caregiving over higher pay').

changes.florida_text: For context, in Florida, minimum wage is $15.00 (as of 2026), which is roughly the equivalent of $2,426 per month for a full-time worker, or about $29,000 per year. However, the average rent price for a one-bedroom apartment in Florida is as low as $1,197 in Tallahassee and around $2,730 in Miami. For a single adult without children, it is estimated that it costs approximately $50,000 annually to cover basic costs of living.
changes.context: A Florida-specific paragraph on minimum wage and cost of living is added with no counterpart in the original. This is new localized content.

changes.original_text: Detroit, once the roaring headquarters of the country's large and profitable automotive industry, had already been in a population decline for several decades as auto manufacturing jobs were being outsourced... Detroit was home to approximately 1.85 million residents in 1950, which dwindled to slightly more than 700,000 in 2010 following the economic crash... The city has filed for bankruptcy, and its unemployment rate hovers around 30 percent.
changes.context: The Detroit bankruptcy case study, used in the original to illustrate deindustrialization and economic decline, is removed from the Florida version.

changes.original_text:
Real Money, Virtual Worlds
If you are not one of the tens of millions gamers who enjoy World of Warcraft, Final Fantasy, Fortnite, Apex, or other online games, you might not know that for some people, these aren't just games: They're employment. The virtual world has been yielding very real profits for entrepreneurs who are able to buy, sell, and manage online real estate, currency, and more for cash...
changes.context: The 'Real Money, Virtual Worlds' sidebar on gaming economies is removed from the Florida version.

changes.original_text: Alongside the debate surrounding what constitutes a family is the question of what people in the United States believe constitutes a marriage. Many religious and social conservatives believe that marriage can only exist between a man and a woman, citing religious scripture and the basics of human reproduction as support. Social liberals and progressives, on the other hand, believe that marriage can exist between two consenting adults—be they a man and a woman, or a woman and a woman—and that it would be discriminatory to deny such a couple the civil, social, and economic benefits of marriage.
changes.florida_text: Debates about marriage definitions continue, ranging from traditional views limiting marriage to a man and a woman, to inclusive perspectives allowing any consenting adults legal recognition and civil benefits.
changes.context: The original's balanced paragraph presenting both conservative and progressive arguments on marriage definition is condensed to a single neutral sentence. The politically charged language — 'discriminatory,' 'social liberals and progressives,' 'religious and social conservatives,' 'religious scripture' — is removed.

changes.original_text: Though other factors such as age and education play a role in these differences, it can be inferred that marriage between parents is generally beneficial for children.
changes.florida_text: Structural factors including economic resources influence the time and energy parents have available to support children. As a result, households with two parents often offer greater financial stability for children.
changes.context: The original's conclusion that marriage is 'generally beneficial for children' is reframed in the Florida version to attribute advantages to economic resources rather than the institution of marriage itself. This shifts the framing from a pro-marriage normative claim toward a structural/economic explanation.
